data.title: Syntactic Systematicity Arising from
data.journal: Proceedings of the Twentieth Annual Conference of the Cognitive Science Society
data.description: A Hebbian-inspired, competitive network is presented which learns to predict the typical semantic features of denoting terms in simple and moderately com-plex sentences. In addition, the network learns to predict the appearance of syntactically key words, such as prepositions and relative pronouns. Importantly, as a by-product of the network's semantic training, a strong form of syntactic systematicity emerges. Moreover, the network can integrate novel nouns and verbs into its training process. This is achieved by assigning predicted semantic features as a default meaning when a novel word is encountered. All network training is unsupervised with respect to error feedback. Issues addressed here have been the subject of debate by notable psy-chologists, philosophers, and linguists within the last decade.
